h. Characters with equal Trait numbers perform their actions simultaneously —

Narrator cans imply choose one to act first for resolution purposes.

Sometimes a character with a high number of Traits will attack a character

h fewer Traits, who will then decide to strike back. In this case, the character

be fewer Traits resolves his action in the same challenge as the faster character,

Sometimes Narrators leave notes on objects, such as books, doors or magic

items that indicate the type of challenges that must be won to achieve a speck

effect with that object (such as deciphering the tome or breaking down the do

Before the test, the Narrator decides on the difficulty of the task in terms

a number of Traits that will need to be beaten in the case of a tic. In the absence

of extenuating circumstances, a difficulty of six Traits is typical, and is a-good

starting point from which Narrators can extrapolate the final difficulty taking in

account the modifiers they know of.

hus using his action for the turn.

This rule can occasionally cause as much confusion as it's trying to solve, and

he Narrator may choose to apply it only when there is a debate or other critical

The test proceeds as with normal challenge, and Ability retests and overbids that

be called as usual. The Narrator may also opt to overbid using the difficulty of the

challenge, which should represent the difficulty and danger inherent in the challenge:

Simple Tests The Challenge Sequence

Simple Tests determine whether you can do something successfully when you Declaration Challenger declares his action and bids a ye Defender

face no real opposition. Simple Tests are often used when using Disciplines, Mast either relents, or declares her response and bids a Trait

Simple Tests do not require you to bid Traits, though some may.

éed to establish who's going first.

Test Players resolve the challenge using Rock-Paper-Scissors.

When a Simple Test is called, a test (Rock-Paper-Scissors) is performed against Compare Traits to resolve ties.

a Narrator. In most cases, the player succeeds on a win or a tie, although it might he 22 Retests Resolve any retests drawn from Abilities, Merits, Willpower or

necessary for an outright win to give the player a successful outcome in some eases:

Order Of Challenges

Typically, all challenges activate simultaneously in a turn. If you absolutely

must have an order of precedence, the simplest method is For a Narrator to pic ka 

character at random and then proceed clockwise around the group, arbitrating

each successful challenge.

Challenge Speeds (Optional Rule)

This rule works only if you trust your players not to misuse the information

revealed from Trait totals for speed in the subsequent challenges of each turn,

supernatural powers

"Lose Traits The character currently in the losing position loses the Trait(s) [oo

they initially bid {in the case of draw, both lose their bid

Traits)

- Overbid The loser of the test can attempt to overbid, depending on the

characters' relative Traits

'Resolution Determine the results of the challenge

It's a fact of life that a large group of characters will decide to mix it up sooner

r later. Group challenges can seem intimidating even to experienced Narrators and

Storytellers. These rules are designed to streamline the resolution process, rather

Since multiple challenges can occur simultaneously during any given fight,

the Narrator occasionally needs some means of determining who acts first and the

acts last. Each person involved in a given turn should check the current number

of Traits that are appropriate to the action he wishes to attempt. Whoever has.the

highest number of appropriate Traits gets to go first. A character punching

someone would count his Physical Traits, while a character using Dementation

would count her Social Traits.

First of all, find out who is challenging whom. The easiest way to do this is to

unit to three and have everyone point to the person they wish to target that turn.

lino one is being challenged by more than one person, then the challenges can be

Bonus Traits from weapons and any applicable penalties are not counted.it

this total, just the Traits in the character's Attribute category. Bonus Traits from

Merits, blood, Disciplines and specializations may be permitted at the discretion.

of the Narrator. Adjustments may need to be made in the case of certain weapons

that allow you to attack before slower weapons. Such cases are specified in hie:

Combat section.

solved normally.

If several characters challenge one target, resolve ir in the following manner.

its, deal with the groups in the order of largest to smallest, just for ease of play.

Each attacker must bid an appropriate Trait as normal for the challenge. For the

are of logistics, no more than five characters can attack another character at once.

Next, the lone character must bid enough Traits to counter every opponent

in the group. If he does nat have enough Traits, he must relent to those opponents

whose bids he cannot meet. He may choose which opponents he relents to. Resolve

those relented challenges first, because the defender may succumb before the rest

If an action does not require any sort of Trait challenge or Trait expenditure,

it comes last in the challenge sequence. If the action requires a simple expenditure

of Willpower or blood, it takes place according to the current Trait level of that

category. Any Discipline effect that has specific Traits attributable to it (e.g-, Am

of Ahriman) uses those specific Traits for their count in this order.

of the pro up can act.

Inn

After the bidding and relenting phase, the defender and his attackers engage:

one simultaneous test. The defender compares his sign to each of the attackers" s

and applies the appropriate results. If the defender throws Scissors and his fourattac

throw Rock, Scissors, Paper and Paper, the defender loses to the first challenger, —

with the second (which is resolved as with any other tie) and wins against the last

The defenders and attackers lose any Traits they have bid ina lost test. If the de fen

in the previous example had bid one Trait against every attacker, he would have, :

one Trait to the first attacker and another if he lost the tie as well.

Once the tests have been made, you resolve them in the standard are

actions and initiative (if using the optional rule). If none of the attackers lose,

defenders imply suffers the results of their actions. It is possible For a defender to

Traits to the first attackers and then lose a tie to an attacker later in the Same me

challenge because of those Traits he lost.

Unless the defender hasa special power that allows him to make multiple action

however, he may attempt to injure only one of his attackers, and it must be one 

lost the challenge against him. If the defender does have extra act on, he care sacrifi

forthcoming actions from that tum to harm additional attackers on a one-for-one was

When the defender in a mob challenge uses Abilities or other powers {0 gal

retests, treat each attacker's challenge as a separate test. Therefore, a defend

would need to spend five Me lee Traits in order to retest against five attackers in

me lee combat challenge if he initially lost against all of them.

Alone Against The World

If character wishes to call a challenge against multiple targets, such as ma kin

amass Social Challenge for performance art or attempting to obfuscate before a to

of people, then he must still undertake a mob challenge. He bids Traits for each

the defenders, up to a maximum of five Traits, no matter the number of defender

Each defender bids a Trait in defense, unless they relent to the challenge. Traits 

lost as normal, but since the attacker has risked no more than five Traits, he cane:

no more than five Traits, no matter how many tests he loses.

Come

Time in Mind's Eye Theatre works as it does in real life. I: moves for wat

inexorably, relentlessly.

For the most part, every action plays out in real time, and players are expect

to stay "in character" unless they have a rules question. A player should nev et do

out of character when interacting with other players. Doing so ruins the: ate

sphere for everyone involved. Players may talk through challenges, bat a char act

is always considered to be active in the game. If a player needs to take a break, A

should inform a Narrator, That player should not interact with any of the the

players while he is out of character. :

The only other exception to this "in-character rule" is when a Narrator calls to

a timeout. This call may be necessary to resolve a dispute or change the scene fifth

story calls for it. When "Timeout!" is called, all players within hearing distance see

stop whatever they are doing until the Narrator calls out "Resume!" or "Lay 08:

Timeouts should be kept to a minimum, since they interrupt the flow of the to

Ra And Big

'(fronicles, Scorics, Sessions And Scenes

- Mind's Eye Theatre time breaks down into five major allotments: chronicles wore

long, scenes and turns. A chronicle is a series of smaller stories that are . connected

chow, and which might take months or even years to complete. Each complete ne

in the chronicle is called a story. A session is one actual night of play, a } you 1

rory tellers may define a session as one night of game time if the act ion ends in media rest at

eendof the previous game. (Since many characters regain spent Traits Renee 'e Signs,

distinction can be important.) Finally, a scene is the time it takes to resolve the - i

né location. Once the characters shift locations, the scene is ended. If session will be

taking place anti rely at one location, a scene can be defined as roughly one hour.

"Tarns are the discrete blocks of time that make up conflicts and scenes. When

years start throwing challenges around or attempting complex actions, it is tine

start using turns to measure game time. Turns last approximately three se cons ,

at this measure may vary from challenge to challenge at the Storyteller's

lis cr et ion. In any given turn a character may take one action (although "me

up er hat ural powers may allow extra actions in a turn). Some actions fa ' P ace

her multiple turns, such as picking a complex lock. Other actions, such Was

eakitig a short sentence, do not take up a character's action at all. Once ever one

solved in a turn has taken an action, that turn ends and the next turn begins.

In'some instances, a character might be interrupted before he can take his

ct ion, or be forced to respond to events developing around him. In such insane

character may always defend himself, although doing so uses up his available action:for the next turn.

Own time

-Many aspect sofa character'sun life are critical to her continued existences ye they

do'not make for dramatic role playing, or they are too intricate to take time ¢ ur ing

sessions to perform. Storytellers are encouraged to use "downtime between sessions to

allow characters to maintain their holdings, learn Disciplines and she to other facets of

their existence. Other actions and interactions may take place during this time, with

Storyteller supervision. As long as players don't abuse downtime privileges, the time

tween sessions can be a rewarding role playing experience in itself.

Each character, Cainite and otherwise, has different health levels that rep re-

Ac the amount of injury the character can endure. A normal character's health

levels: are: Healthy, Healthy, Bruised, Bruised, Bruised, Wounded, Wound es

incapacitated, Torpor and/ or Final Death. Mortals replace Torpor with Mortal 

= — Wounded. If a fully uninjured character loses two health levels, she becomes

a Bruised. If she loses three more health levels, she becomes Wounded, and so on.

= Certain merits or supernatural powers may alter this typical health track.

As a character's condition worsens, it becomes harder to carry out various

ct ions. Vampires are more resilient than mortals, but they can still be injured or

content:
even killed with enough damage. The following penalties apply to the char ac

: :

depending on the level of health at which he currently sits. Note that the ch allen:

penalties apply to all types of challenges. a

Healthy —- When a character is healthy, he is virtually or complete

uninjured. He suffers no penalty aside from possibly being scraped or rumpled

Bruised — A Bruised character ism ore seriously roughed up, and his injuries ha

started to impair his viability. He receives a one-Trait penalty on all tied challenge:

Wounded -- When a character is Wounded, he is seriously injured in one

more locations. To reflect this level of injury, he is one Trait down on 4

challenges, and his opponent wins all ties, reg arc less of who has more Traits, l£th

injured character has a power that normally allows him to win ties appropriate 

the challenge, ties ave resolved through comparing Traits instead, Note: a cha rae

her may always attempt to overbid.

Incapacitated — When a character is Incapacitated, he is completely out of play

for at least Lo minutes. After thar time, and again awake, the character is still effective

immobile, although he may whisper pained sentences. He cannot initiate am

challenges until he has healed at least one health level. He is at the mercy of the

characters, and he may do nothing more than heal himself. He will require assistance

to move. Mortals injured beyond this point are Mortally Wounded.

Torpor —Cainites injured beyond Incapacitated by bashing or lethal dam ag

fall into a deathlike sleep known as torpor.

Mortally Wounded — A mortal injured beyond incapacitation has little time left.

He does not regain consciousness normally and loses Physical Trait every 10 minutes

As soon as he runs out of Physical Traits, he dies. If he takes any damage, bashing 1

lethal, during this time, he dies instantly. Few things can rescue a mortal at this point

Final Death — Cainites injured beyond Incapacitated by aggravated damian

do not enter or remain in torpor — they are permanently destroyed. At the

~ slloawe ce he era tie a aft see ' i

Storytellers discretion, significant quantities of bashing or lethal damage (suffix

client to dismember or destroy the corpse) may also cause Final Death.

Damage Types

Characters can suffer injury from a broad range of sources. A character!

physical nature determines his vulnerability to injury and the degree to which 

is affected. A Cainite can shrug off injury that would cripple a mortal, yet sustain

damage from sources that a human would't consider a risk at all. The Narrator!

the final arbiter of which category of damage a specific event causes:

Bashing Damage

Bashing damage is unlikely to kill, and even mortals heal it swiftly. Bruises, sprains and 28

minor injuries such as those from falls, punches or clubs are included. Cainites are. largely

unaffected by such minor injuries due to the lack of blood in their body tissues, so theysuffe only half (round down, minimum of one level) the indicated damage from such Causes:

While bashing damage is ra rely fatal, it does soften the victim up for worse ©

come. If a Cainite is reduced to Incapacitated by bashing damage, subsequent

aggravated damage will cause Final Death. Additional bashing or lethal dam ag

wills imply place them in torpor. =

or Incapacitated mortals, any additional bashing damage converts current

health levels from bashing into lethal damage as bones break and organs crush.

my take any level of actual lethal damage, they become Mortally Wounded, as

hey will die if subsequent bashing damage pushes them past Incapacitated again.

== Example: Joshua has been beaten senseless by thugs, and he lies Incapacitated in the

| Francis, his rival for the hand of ala dy, takes the opportunity to plant a boot in Joshua's

This act causes one level of bashing damage normally, but it converts Joshua's first lost

Thy level into a lethal loss. Joshua must now nurse 4 broken rib, rather than just tending

his bruises. If Francis had instead stabbed his vital with a dagger, Joshua would be dying...

Eth al Damage

As the name suggests, lethal damage is far more serious, and it has the potential

ill or maim, especially if the victim is mortal. Most bladed weapons inflict lethal

a, as might some blunt weapons (noted in their individual descriptions).

Unlike bashing damage, lethal damage has a much more immediate effect on

totals. Any mortal reduced below Incapacitated by lethal damage is dying. A

nite in a similar position is simply driven into torpor, but be does not reach

nail Death as a result. Such injuries still make the vampire vulnerable to Final

each through subsequent aggravated damage, though.

Ag gr av aced Damage

Although Cainites are far more resilient in general than mortals, some forms

jury-are dire even to them. Largely elemental or supernatural in origin (e.g.,

fire, sunlight, the teeth and claws of other vampires, or any other source the

Jat rat or deems sufficient due to circumstances}, these sources of injury are hard

6 resist and equally hard to heal.

Mortals suffer lethal damage from those things that would cause a Cainite to take

pera vat ed damage (except sunlight, of course). There are some specific instances in

ice a mortal may receive aggravated damage, however, and they typically require

et natural means to heal them. These are explained on.a case-by-case basis.

Sunlight

_ Exposure to sunlight is extremely painful for vampires. Indirect sunlight, such

s through thick curtains or heavy cloud cover causes one automatic aggravated

and per turn of exposure, while direct sunlight causes three levels per turn and

'ob ably causes the vampire to burst into flame. Even gazing into a sunlit area may

use temporary blindness as the reflected rays burn the vampire's eyes. Heavy

thing provides some protection, reducing damage or the rate at which it is

fer ed, at the Storyteller's discretion. Vampires confronted by sunlight must also

are a Courage Test for Rétschreck.

Fire

Cainites fear and loathe fire, and its slightest touch causes them great agony.

Out vampires are uncomfortable around open flame, since it can destroy them

ethanently, Sufficiently large sources of flame inevitably provoke Courage Tests

Cainites to avoid Rétschreck.

li game terms, all normal and supernatural fires cause aggravated damage to Cainites.

it also circumvents protective armor. Fire will cause a level of damage relative to the

2 'Empire's exposure to it. A torch will typically cause one level of ago ra vat ed damage with

Applying Damage

The three types of damage (hashing, lethal and aggravated) are cu mu la tiv

and the penalties she suffers. Different wound types heal at different rates both

level of the health track to the first until the character is completely Healthy again

Example: A lone hunter attacks Marie, a Malkavien. The fight is somewhat one-side

During the scuffle, however, the hunter managed to inflict several blows on Marie specific al

two sword blows, three separate pommel strikes and nasty han from an car by candle's flam

Healing

Cain ice Healing

Vampires heal through the simple expenditure of blood. Such an action

reflexive and takes no actions to accomplish, although the Cainite is still restrict?

to the limits of his blood expenditure as per his generation. Healing.a level

bashing or lethal damage takes the expenditure of one Blood Trait. "

Example: If Marie expends four Blood Traits over the next few turns to heal, she

he Bruised, but she is still suffering a lethal wound and an aggravated wound (taking

lace of her two Healthy levels). Later that night, after feeding, she then heals the

na in ing lethal wound with one more Blood Trait.

In order to heal one level of aggravated damage, the Cainite must spend three

god Traits. The damage heals over the following day as the vampire sleeps. This

sod can be expended over a period of several days of rest — simply keep a note

he character sheet of how much blood has been expended for the health leveling healed. If the vampire wishes to attempt to heal more than one level of

ava ted damage in one day, she must expend a Willpower Trait for each

(dit ion al aggravated health level sal ed in the same rest period (in addition to

e three Blood Traits per aggravated health level).

Mortal Healing

Mortals are much more fragile and take longer to heal than vampires. The

medical expertise of the Dark Medieval is also quite primitive compared to modern

di cine, and the following rules take this into account. The following table

udicates the length of time a mortal must spend recovering from injury. Each

earth level must be healed individually, so a mortal who was reached Incapaci-

ed from bashing damage must spend one week recovering to the Wounded level,

a further three days recovering to Bruised.

ain factor is time, although medical attention may accelerate the process.

fe ct ion. Scars are commonplace. Characters injured with lethal damage to the

oral health level each day to reflect infection and blood loss. After initial medical tent ion, further treatment is not required, so long as the character is above

sounded. Incapacitated characters require constant medical attention and are rob ably delirious or unconscious.

Drugs And Poisons

Mortals use a variety of substances and herbs to heal, harm Gr alter percept io:

Most cannot be used directly by Cainites, though they may be imbibed: indirect

through the blood of unwitting or victims or. willing vessels. Rather than detailing!

effect of every drug and poison, the following are the most common and typical effete

Alcohol — A vampire drinking blood that contains alcohol suffers some

the effects of being drunk. For every two drinks consumed by the victim, t

Cainite gains the Negative Physical Trait Cheesy and the Negative Mental The

Oblivious. This effect dissipates at the rate of one Negative Trait pet hourorscen

Bloodroot (Sanguinaria) — The rare and deadly bloodroot is one.of the 

poisons that is directly harmful to Cainites. Consuming it (for example, mixed i

blood) causes three health levels of aggravated damage, while using it asa . count

poison (such as on a blade) inflicts two health levels of aggravated dam ag addition to any injury caused by the weapon. The poison attacks vampiric ¥

turning if into an ash like black dust.

Food Poisoning (Salmonella} — Blood tainted with salmonella causes nous

and a level of bashing damage per Blood Trait consumed to the feeding vamp!

Additionally, she has difficulty drinking blood until purged of the bad blood(

under "Diseases"), but she may force herself to drink (see'the feeding rules and

"Plague"). The effects of food poisoning wear off after about a day.

Hashish — In common use in the Middle East, hashish has a calming effect;

ital so affects the imbiber's attentiveness. A vampire drinking hashish-tainted blood

gains the Negative Mental Trait Oblivious. Hashish also reduces the difficulty oe

resisting frenzy by one Trait. These effects last for approximately one hour:

Hallucinogens -— Hallucinogenic drugs such as cowbane, hen ban¢e 2

deadly nightshade (belladonna) are used as both painkillers and poisons in

Dark Medieval. A vampire drinking blood that contains stall quantities of so

herbs is considered one to three Traits down (depending on the amount cons um

by the victim and vampire) in any challenges because of impaired con central

he character also suffers effects equivalent to Haunt the Soul. Other effects occur

the Storyteller's discretion, but they may include gaining temporary Basic level

'Auspex or losing a Trait of Courage. The effects of hallucinogens last For a number

f hours, possibly until the end of the game session, but they may clear if there is

«nificant time lapse between two scenes involving the character.

Painkillers -—— As with hallucinogens, herbs such as hemlock, mandrake and

nkshood are used as both painkillers and poisons. In small doses, they reduce the

disabling effects of injury, dropping the wound penalty for lost health levels.

bruised characters have no penalties, Wounded characters suffer Bruised penal-

is, Incapacitated characters suffer Wounded penalties. Allot her health levels are

affected. The effects last as for hallucinogens.

Poison — Larger doses of hallucinogens or painkillers, or extracts such as

end¢, strychnine or cyanide, can prove fatal to mortals and harmful to Cainites.

While a mortal might suffer an agonizing death, a Cainite is down one to three Traits

all challenges and takes between one and three health levels of lethal damage,

Depending on the strength of the poison and the amount of tainted blood he's drunk.

sons cannot kill Cainites or drive them into torpor, but they can temporarily

A character's mental state is as important as her physical health, and it is much less controllable in many cases. A vampire can use blood points to heal a physical

found, but mental injuries and states are more difficult to deal with.

Frenzy And Rocs chr eck

ln many ways an ultimate illustration of the "fight-or-flight" reflex, the Beast lurks

neath the surface of all vampires and manifests as a vicious form of madness when

ought forth. Most Cainites resist the Beast desperately, straining to keep it under

ntrol, When they fail, the resulting orgy of instinctual violence can level friends and

es alike — the Beast does not discriminate. The vampire feels no pain and acts on

- — thstinct, using his bare hands and fangs to rip apart anything around him. Usually the

= fusttargetisthe creature that triggered the frenzy, or anything and anyone between him

nd his target, Otherwise, the closest objector creature to hand will suffer.

The triggers of the Beast are often highly personalized, but triggers common

all vampires are blood hunger, extreme passion, frustration, defeat, humiliation

t the hands of a rival or threats to a vampire's friends or loved ones. Players must

consistent with what causes a frenzy in their characters; a player cannot ignore

om eth ing that would normally trigger a frenzy simply because it in't convenient

to him to frenzy at this time. The Beast doe sn't care. Discretion is advised,

ow ever; a character in constant frenzy is no fun to be around and will likely be "put

own" by other vampires.

Succumbing to the Beast is considered shameful and debased among many

Ai nites, and it might result in the character losing status. Vampires will be held

Fe sp on sib le for their frenzied actions by other vampires. The social repercussions

May be worse than the situation that brought forth the Beast in the first place.

Frenzy

A character may expend a Willpower Trait to fend off frenzy for 10 miiniite

If the trigger is still present at the end of that time, however, the vampire ing

gain for frenzy immediately or spend another Willpower Trait for another

10 minutes.

Fighting the Beast

if character resists entering frenzy, the player performs as

. If the vampire succeeds in the test, he manages to push aside

Beast takes hold. Once in frenzy, the character may expend a Willpower Traits 

attempt an additional Self-Control Test to regain his sanity, ending the frenzy.

Riding the Beast

A character with the Instinct Virtue will always enter frenzy, unless she has

-more Instinct Traits than double the rating of the stimulus. In this case, she may

choose whether or not to frenzy.

Example: Catherine has an Instinct Virtue of four Traits. If someone

taunts her (a two-Trait trigee « the Beast, she will frenzy. Ifs 

in Instinct, she would have the option to hold her Beast in rein.

Once in frenzy, she can opt to make an Instinct Test (as with See

above) to control her actions for one turn. If she wins this test, she manages

direct her frenzy for the turn, although she still suffers all the usual conditions of

frenzy (see the box that follows). Ifs he loses, her frenzy are as normal. In the

event of a tie, she compares her Instinct Traits to the provocation rating:to

determine the outcome.

Example: Catherine, ripping into her tormenter, decides that she needs to have.

a difficulty of two Traits (the rating of the trigger). She ties, but compares her Traits with

the difficulty, easily om ing the challenge (four Traits to two). Her fist drawn back,

she we en words in his ear... and the Beast roars back on the next tu,

spurring her hand forward...

Coming Down

Once the trigger is removed (or destroyed or dead), the character gets onett

Control Instinct Test against the same provocation difficulty to come out of

frenzy. Otherwise, the frenzy burns out on its own 10 minutes later, leaving the

Cainite lucid at last to deal with the mess.

Humiliation.

During the course of role playing, it should become clear if a Cainite is being

humiliated to the point of frenzy. Devil aunts can stir the Beast quite Quick :

If role playing cannot resolve the situation, then a player can call For a Social

Challenge to resolve the matter. If the taunting player wins, then the defender must

check for frenzy. If the defender wins, not

All vampires have some level of control over this te , and the trigger

needs'to present a clear and immediate danger to the vampire in her for it to

ring thar the nights of the Dark Medieval

When in frenzy, a Cainite has the following .

advantages and disadvantages:

* He ignores all wound penalties until he reaches torpor or Final Death. -

* He cannot initiate Social Challenges unless that challenge is related _

to intimidation. ;

© He does not need to risk any Social Traits if a Social Challenge is

called against him.

® He is immune to further-checks for frenzy or Rotschreck.

we

Duran

world are lit with naked flames. While the presence of these light sources \We

definitely put the vampire on edge, they will only initiate Rétschreck if they

towards the Cainite. Large fires nearby will always shake the confidence of 

vampire, and any attempts to resist frenzy if there is as mall campfire or greater sized

source of flame nearby will make the test one Trait more difficult than usual:

To avoid Rétschreck, the vampire must make a Static Courage Challenge

(Courage versus the Narrator's difficulty, no Traits risked). Systemically, Rats chr eck

is identical to frenzy for the purposes of determining duration, except that Courage

is used rather than Self-Control. You cannot use the rules for riding the Beast in the 2

case of Rétschreck, however. All vampires must fight the Beast or flee in frenzy.

A vampire entering Rétschreck. flees the object. of her fear as directly:and rapidly as she can, blindly attacking any obstacles or people that get in her way, of

flight proves impossible, she will attempt to destroy the object of her fear then flee

If such options are not available, she curls up in abject terror for the duration of the

Rétschreck. Note that this fear does not override her self-preservation instincts

she will not leap from a tower's height to avoid a candle —- but any path that.is not

obviously suicidal becomes an option.

Without hope, many Cainites would give themselves to the Beast and revel

in their monstrous nature. Various paths of control are available to vampires to

quell their Beast, and a number of philosophies have been suggested as to how to

finally bind the Beast in a position of total submission. Cainite my ch ology. espouses

the state of Golconda, a legendary state of transcendence that reflects mastery over

the Beast. Rumors exist of those who have achieved such a glorious goal, but they,

usually take the form of "a friend of a friend," with no direct witnesses: :

Many vampires on the Road of Heaven have sought Golconda, following 

loose process known as the Bitter Journey, but few, if any, have attained it; With

the growth of the presence of devout Christian, Muslim and Jewish. vampires, -

Golconda is becoming condemned as a pagan practice, at odds with the concept

of God's redemption. Indeed, more fanatical Cainites have been known to'hunt

down and slay heretics rumored to be undertaking the Bitter Journey.

Whether Golconda exists and is achievable is up to individual Storytellers, There

are no fixed systems for Golconda due to its inherently ephemeral nature. Each Cainite

must discover her own path to enlightenment — and Storytellers are encouraged to be

extremely demanding when it comes to Golconda quests. This journey can easily take

up years of play and constant progress through multiple stories. The only solid rule is

that the Cainite must maintain a Road rating and Conscience rating of at least out

Traits to remain on the path to Golconda. Slipping below that level will generally cause

mentors on the path to desert the Cainite until he atones for his degeneration:

The following philosophies offer guidelines for such a quest to be part of .

chronicle. One or more of these effects may be granted to a character who has clearly indicated through excellence in role play over the course of his quest that

he has achieved control in that area. :

Acceptance: Those who have attained Golconda accept themselves for what

they are. They do not rail on about their "damned" status, nor.do. they sulfet

jealousy of mortal life. They ares imply part of existence.

Calm: Those who attain Golconda are reputed to have mastered their Beast

ad never fear succumbing to frenzy or Roétschreck, including immunity to

sciplines that cause such effects. ;

Hunger: Although they are still tied to the requirements of blood, a Cainite

tho has attained Golconda needs to feed much less often (perhaps only required

a spend one blood point per week, rather than per day).

Strength: A Cainite who has attained Golconda is not fully bound by the

hackles of generation, so she may learn Disciplines in excess of her usual limits.

Alb other generational limits remains unaffected.

Mortality: Perhaps the most seductive of the legends associated with Geleonda

st hat, through enlightenment, a vampire may throw off Caine's curse and return

'o mortality. How such a transformation might be effected is spoken of only in

whispers, but self-sacrifice, true love and devotion are recurring themes. Even

those who believe in Golconda usually regard these stories of transformation as

fanciful, but as always, the truth of the matter is in the hands of the Storyteller.

Che Blood Oath

One of the strangest properties of Cainite vitae is its ability to forge a link

between the vampire and the drinker. This power is used to create mortal servants,

but it can also be used to bind other vampires to the Cainite's will. The strength

of this bond depends on the number of times the victim has drank the blood of the

same-Cainite, but after three such sips, the drinker is almost irrevocably bound.

The person (living or unliving) who comes under this bond, called the blood oath,

is known as the thrall, while the Cainite holding power over them is the remnant.

A fully bound thrall is utterly devoted to her regnant and cannot take action

against her. In many cases, this feeling is like a mortal addiction or obsession. The

thrall: may not necessarily love his regnant (though many do}, and he may even

actively despise her, but he follows her orders nonetheless, even if he is dominated or otherwise manipulated by outside agencies. Many Cainites have described the

oath as a compelling force, but it is not necessarily a compulsion to do exactly as

the regnant says. Rather the thrall is honor-bound to demonstrate loyalty, to obey

orders and the express wishes of the regnant. This distinction between compelled

obedience and loyalty is a fine line, but it allows the thrall to demonstrate some

flexibility. Invariably, however, the thrall follows his master's wishes, the way of

oath-bound loyalty in the Dark Medieval for mortals and Cainites alike. Going

against the master's wishes is simply unconscionable.

The power of the oath is not dependent on the regnant's generation. The

good of a 12th-generation neonate can bind an elder as readily as the elder binds

others to her will. This is the only innate Cainite power that does not appear to

degrade with generation. A Cainite may be fully oath bound to only one regnant,

but she may have several thralls and lesser bonds (those involving one or two

lt inks) herself.

Caking The Oath

The most common way to undergo the full oath or even the single drink is in

4 Ceremony of outright fealty. This ceremony involves a gathering of a Cainite

court, at which point the thrall drinks from the regnant's slit wrist and Sw:

loyalty to her lord. The lord also swears to protect his new vassal ag if she we

his own blood. It is from this practice that the oath gets its name.

Most of the Darned ares imply too willful to surrender. them se lye

permanently and utterly to another, so very few Cainites ever come under the

effect of the blood oath. Nevertheless, there are vampires who were ghouls be

their Embrace (and thus carry on their blood oath), and the chains of Cain

feudalism can be strengthened by the power of the oath. :

There are three steps in the process, corresponding to the three drinks (w

must be at least a night apart) the victim has taken from another character.

First Drink: At this stage, the drinker finds herself drawn to the other cain

and has strong flashes of emotion regarding her regnant. She might:day dr 3

about the vampire, or her subconscious might seek out "coincidental" encounter

There are no game effects at this point, but it should be role played.

Second Drink: She now definitely considers the vampire to be a central i

it difficult to act against him (win or tie a Simple Test to take an action ditec

harmful to the regnant), and she may be commanded easily by the regnant:-(o

Trait down to resist the regnant's Social Challenges against her). :

regnant. The intense passion of the weaker bonds fades, replaced by a strong sense =

of loyalty and belonging. She has a sense chat she is in harmony with her own lodz=

existence. All others become secondary to her regnant. An oath bound thrall¢

be influenced with Dominate by her regnant without the need of eye contact = 2 =

hearing her master's voice is enough, and the thrall is two Traits down to resist

challenge. (Generation still applies to Dominate attempts, however.)

Oachbreaking

A thrall may resist the bond temporarily by spending a Willpower Trait. Th

bond may be resisted for one whole scene if the action taken is relatively ind ire

while one Willpower Trait is required per sem if the thrall wishes to physically 2 =

attack her regnant.

The blood oath may lapse or be broken with time, bur doing so requites.a long

period of separation between regnant and thrall and considerable will power: As =

rough guide, if the thrall abstains from all contact with her regnant For a period of in

equal to 12 minus her permanent Willpower Traits, the binding power of the a

weakens by one level. If the thrall was grossly mistreated by the regnant this: Heme

requirement may decrease at the Storyteller's discretion. During this/time,

character must role play the painful process of separation as well as spending Will pow

Traits to battle the "addiction" she has to her regnant (Narrator's discretion):

The blood oath also ends if the regnant dies, though the effect of this breaking val le

from thrall to thrall. Some claim to feel a sense of great elation and joy when the bone 2

breaks, while others feel psychosomatic pain. Such matters are solely at the discretion

the Storyteller, Regardless, the regnant feels no such effects if the thrall dies.

we

At the Storyteller's discretion, a character who possesses the Merit: True Love to

one other than her regnant may be able to break the bond with the power of her

, but doing so requires arduous, in-depth role playing and Storyteller approval.

/Er an ge men ts

Mental trauma, boredom due to centuries of tedium or continual loss —- all

'see factors and many more can lead to the twisting of a Cainite's perception of

city; Madness is very real part of the Dark Medieval, and those who suffer from

are variously seen as cursed or blessed. A Storyteller may decide that 

rangement is in order after any experience that generates especially intense and

pleasant emotions, or which see rely violates the character's beliefs or ethics.

While much of this section is written about Cainites, mortals can likewise

fer derange men ts, though the Storyteller may need to alter aspects of the rules

video. For example, mortals cannot enter frenzy, but they can attack wildly with

impleve disregard for their actions and the well-being of those around them.

All derange men ts carry "triggers" — circumstances that cause the effects of

e derangement to surface. These triggers should be assigned appropriate to the

experience that caused the derangement, or they might be dependant on the

inure of the derangement itself. Once triggered, the derangement becomes active

eth ed ur at ion of the scene, and the player must modify her character's Traits,

titudes and behaviors in accordance with the derangement's description.

De tan ge men ts may be resisted by the expenditure of a Willpower Trait. Such 

sp en is ion lasts for only the duration of the scene, however, and if the trigger is still present

the end of that time, the character must expend another Willpower Trait of suffer the

acts.of the derangement, Narrators may rule that additional Willpower Traits or

ill power Challenges (treat asa normal challenge, risking Willpower Traits rather than

attributes) may be required to deal with particularly intense mental stresses.

The quest to cure derange men ts is fuel for long, intense stories, and the solution of such stories rests solely in the hands of the Storyteller. Modern

ychotherapeutic methods are hardly accessible in the Dark Medieval, and

serous bizarre and tortuous methods were conceived to rid the insane of their

devilment. Indeed, many insane people were considered to be possessed by the

Pet natural, and overtly dangerous lunatics sometimes drew unwanted attention

om the Church. Malkavians may never cure the original derangement(s) brought

n by the clan disadvantage, but they may work at shedding others that have

th er wise sprung from their experiences.

Here is brief list of derange men ts and their effects on characters. Storytellers might

h to allow other derange men ts into play, but they are advised to develop rules such as

here to avoid confusion over their in-game effects in the future. Often frenzy can he the

Result of actual reality jarring the deranged character, and a Self-Control Instinct Test is

required, with a difficulty decided by the Narrator based on the circumstances.

Note: There is nothing funny or arbitrary about the way a "crazy" person acts. To

i, his behavior is perfectly normal, and he is simply reacting to the stimuli that he

te ives tore real. Players should never forget the Mind's Eye Theatre rules of safety

when tole playing derange men ts. Playing derange men ts involves advanced role playing

ill'and is better suited to experienced players. Additionally, derange men ts are

This is one good reason to thoroughly discuss and document the sec

Amnesia we

In highly traumatic situations, the character sometimes forgets who:

what he is. This typically occurs when he comes face-to-face with his we

condition. The character mights imply forget the memory of a singlé Bra 

he might forget everything about his identity, including his true nat ut 4

even forget Abilities or Disciplines and be surprised to discover that 

things he thought were beyond his capabilities. When events and situate

might remind him of his lost memories present themselves, those merge

return, sometimes doing so violently and sending the character-into a frénzy

Bulimia :

. Bulimic characters salve their guilt and insecurity by over-indulging

activities that comfort them — in this instance, consuming food (or blood

vampires). Characters with this affliction will gorge themselves as much as pass

when under stress, then purge their system through drastic means (burn in

Traits, Disciplines or regurgitating blood as described in "Starve a Cold?)

consume more. Characters with this derangement must make a Static Conscie

Conviction Challenge when feeding. Fat lure means that the vampire feeds until

has reached bis maximum Blood Trait capacity, whether or not he needs the be

If forcibly kept from feeding, he must resist frenzy.

Crimson Rage

A character with this derangement is prone to experiencing fits of anger we

little provocation. While the two bear a certain resemblance, this state is u

different from frenzy, Frenzy is the instincts of the vampiric Beast, while Crime

Rage is the vampire's own feelings of helplessness and inadequacy. He is £

protected from being pushed over the edge into frenzy while in the grip oft I

derangement, however. Whenever this derangement is active, the char act eg 

the Negative Mental Traits: Violent 2 and Impatient.

Fantasy/Delusion

. Some characters cannot accept the real world, so they transpose th ems el

into an illusory world instead. As this is something of a "catch-all" derange me

the scope and degree of this fantasy varies considerably, and details should be for

out with the Storyteller. While the character interacts with people and institution'

of the Dark Medieval in an almost normal manner, her perception of event

skewed by her fantasy, Something happening to shatter the illusions the character

has crafted about herself can cast her into frenzy.

Fugue

Characters suffering this affliction react to stress by adopting a specific set al

behaviors. In the process, they suffer "blackouts" or periods of memory loss. Whenever

the character is confronted by extreme stress, the player must win a Static Willpoy

Challenge against a difficulty determined by che Narrator depending of the degree «

stress, Failure means that the character blacks out and the player must role play th

meters trance like state. Otherwise, control of the character passes to a Narrator for

one, who dictates the actions the character takes in order to remove the stress. At

ad oft he fugue, the character "regains consciousness" with no memory of his actions.

Hysteria

Hysterical characters are unable to control their emotions, and they suffer

severe mood swings and fits of intense violence. A hysterical Cainite will

y more easily than other vampires (increase the difficulty of his Self-Control/

din ct Test by one Trait), particularly when faced with great stress. In addition,

time the vampire fails in a particularly stressful or prominent situation.

ar rat or's call) the character will automatically enter frenzy, with no check.

Immortal Terror

This madness stems from the vampire's inability to deal with the true scope

his immortality. Terrified by the real implications of living forever, the vampire

pes by developing a strong unconscious "death wish." Whenever he is con-

ont ed: by direct evidence of his immortality — such as attending a funeral or

thing a mortal ally die — the character must make a Static Willpower

hal len ge (against four Traits) to avoid immediately taking actions that might

suit in his destruction, Such actions can be as indirect as breaking the Silence of

¢ Blood by confessing to a priest, as long as the act carries potentially deadly

mn sequences. Note that the vampire is not consciously aware that he seeks his

destruction, and he resists attempts to persuade him otherwise.

- Lu macy

This madness comes and goes, linked to the cycle of the moon. When the

soon is full, the character is manic and delirious, while at the time of the new

on; he may be reserved and melancholic. At intervening times, he may appear

otmal and unaffected. While in the grip of this derangement, his ability to resist

envy and Rétschreck varies accordingly, increasing or decreasing by a Trait to

effect his mood depending on his link to the lunar cycle.

Manic-Depression

A:Cainite with this derangement experiences wild mood swings. She are-

entry slips into deep depression, losing interest in her normal activities and

becoming withdrawn. Alternately, she is gripped by bursts of fervent energy. When

- suffering from depression, the vampire's Willpower Traits are greatly reduced (half,

found up, the normal value — when the phase ends, Willpower Traits return to

hit previous level, minus any spent during that period). These depressive periods

= often follow failure of a particular action, though they may also result from hunger

{blood pool of one-quarter orle ss) or psychological factors. Hyperactivity will see

the vampire working for hours or even into the day on her projects, resisting the

=~ teed to sleep as she burns up blood and Willpower on her schemes. She is also one

Trait down to resist frenzy during this manic time. Should someone attempt to stir

depressive or thwart a manic vampire, the vampire may frenzy.

Megalomania

Characters with this derangement believe that they are destined to lead, and

they seek to accumulate power, irrespective of their skill and the attitude of others.

They believe that those who dismiss their claims are jealous, seeking to hold onto

Power and deny them their dues. Megalomaniacs believe that these opponents

Visions :

This derangement leads sufferers to believe that they are granted an in sigh tint

the divine through visions, trances and other ecstatic states, During these spells;to :

may be catatonic, in a trance like state or rave uncontrollably. Their ability to 're cal

details of the visions is similarly varied, sometimes recalling precise details.while =

These visions could be products of an overactive imagination, or they could jat the 222

Che Amaranth (Diablerie)

The Curse of Caine is diluted through the thinning of vitae within the graduate

generations from the progenitor, so the blood of elder vampires is more potent than th

of their childer. This power can be stolen, though, by consuming the blood and'soil 6

the elder, This act ~- the Amaranth (which vampires refer to as "diablerie") joey

considered akin to cannibalism among mortals, and it is one of the most heinous chime

av amp ire can commit. Although the Assamites of the Levant practice the ritual diablerie

of their enemies or the Lasombra may sanction the Amaranth of sire by cbildle (with th

approval of their elders), and some say the Tremere are predisposed to soul-drinking; Most

other vampires balk at the thought of committing such an unforgivable sin, we

Committing Amaranth

Note: You cannot diablerie anything but another vampire. While you can drat =

almost any other creature of blood and even kill it, the Amaranth is strictly ac ai nite delicacy

The process of Amaranth is divided into four distinct stages. Celerity can ne

be used to hasten this foul deed after the first stage. :

First, the diablerist must incapacitate his target. Only after the target 

physically immobilized may diablerie commence.

The diablerist. then drains his victim of vital, automatically taking one Bk

Trait per turn. Yes, this is slower than normal feeding. It's a different process, after al

Now the true Amaranth begins. The diablerist then continues to drain th

vitality of bis target, ina similar manner to how the Beast degenerates the body 0

a hungry vampire, sucking the last of his juices and stripping away any remain,

health levels -—- an action called "drinking the heart's blood." To do so, the take

is engaged in a series of Physical Challenges, against which she may bid of!

mage can not be reduced with Fortitude. The target is still mobile and conscious

the is above Incapacitated but locked into the Amaranth. If the diablerist ever

loses a draining challenge, then he falls away from the body and fails the attempt.

During this phase, no Mental or Social Challenges can target the diablerist, but be

oc an not defend himself physically. Any external attempts to attack him or pull

him away succeed automatically.

Once the victim is drained dry of blood and reduced to torpor, the final

struggle begins, as the diablerist tries to ensnare the victim's soul before it escapes.

This effort is a Physical Challenge, and the victim is up three Traits on each

challenge (non-cumulative). The diablerist may continue trying until he can no

on ger match the victim's Physical Traits. Success means the diablerist has won.

The victim's body immediately begins to decay as its essence merges with that of

the diablerist. Failure means that che target's spirit flees into Final Death.

A character may have other characters assist him in incapacitating his target

ad ce ven in draining the target of Blood Traits and health levels, but only one

Cainite can gain the ultimate benefits of the Amaranth, The seduction of the

process is very strong, though. A character who wishes to halt diablerie after

entering the third stage must spend a Willpower Trait and succeed in a Static

Mental Challenge against three Traits, or she will attempt to continue.

Halting Diablerie

No challenge required

felt. of the Amaranth, a sensation to which some Cainites become addicted. The

others instead of the good in themselves. True Faith is selfless, hopeful, dedicated

nd above all, rare. Still, in a time when the Church is both a spiritual and

temporal power, there are enough true believers and holy people in the world to

are True Faith a palpable threat to Cainites everywhere.

example, an 11th-generation vampire drinking the blood and soul of a meriher 9

the Ninth Generation). In such a case, the diablerist effectively perm ane nth

lowers his generation by one (i.c., gain a Trait in the Generation Background),

If the victim was of much greater power (five or more generations), the di abler is

may gain more than one generation, though this decision is ultimately up to'th

Storyteller. Alternatively or additionally, if the victim was sufficiently powerful, th

diablerist may experience a brief surge in his Disciplines (one or two levels in one

two Disciplines, generally). Any Discipline surges are granted solely at the Storyteller'

discretion, and in no event do these bonuses last for more than one scene.

Che Thorn

Many young vampires believe diablerie to be the perfect crime. The body 

destroyed in the process, making it difficult to secure enough evidence to support

an accusation of murder. Diablerists quickly learn, however, that there are ways 0

identifying those who have committed this heinous sin. Storytellers are encour

aged to remember the horrific magnitude of the crime and ensure that diablerie

never becomes a casual crime.

For most Europeans, this faith is in Jesus Christ, but other faiths are equally

powerful. Jews, Muslims, pagans and even heretics can have as much faith. (or

more) than Christians. The truth of the belief is not as important as the strength

of the faithful's conviction.

© = Most Cainites will only come across True Faith when they do battle with the

Inquisition or other witch-hunters. Only mortals may possess True Faith — the

Embrace strips a vampire of the purity required for it. Those vampires on the Road

'of Heaven who devoutly believe may occasionally touch what surrounds the blessed.

During the course of a story, a character may be able to develop True Faith,

although this should be the basis for an ongoing major subplot for the character.

Characters may improve their faith only with exceptional role playing and Story-

teller approval. The game uses for True Faith are covered exhaustively in Laws of

"the Hunt (characters with the Merit: True Faith from that game are considered to

® First, and most importantly, a character on the Via Hum ani tat is always lose: He Trait of Faith). S basic rules for faith are included | though

"have one Trait of Faith). Some basic rules for faith are included here, though.

at least one Humanity Trait permanently for committing this act, and he mightlas

even more if the deed was especially foul — no test, no appeal. Even character

following another road stand to lose a Road Trait for such a horrible crime, unless *

their road specifically condones it.

By brandishing his holy symbol, the bearer gains the Social Trait: Intimidating

when repelling the undead and warding off supernatural powers. If it is used to

strike a supernatural creature, a holy symbol grants the extra Physical Traits

a . . a, : Burning and Searing, and it inflicts one level of aggravated damage.

¢ A Cainite using the Auspex power of Soul sight may detect di able tie:ing and Searing, and it is sone le Be ra vat ed Cam as

vampire's aura for up to three months after the diablerie was committed. Evidence

of this crime reveals itself as black veins running through the offender's aura:

¢ Likewise, a vampire using the Thaumaturgy power of Taste for Blood may detect

diablerie the character has committed, regardless of the centuries since the act:

~~ A character with True Faith may attempt to repel vampires by brandishing his holy symbol, invoking his belief ("In the name of _, Command you to be gone?"') and

calling For a Social Challenge. Success means that the vampire must make a test for

Rotschreck or flee the area immediately. Failure means the vampire must still back off

few feet and cower before taking action, and the vampire may not initiate any attacks

_ during the next turn, although she may still defend herself normally. A vampire may

_tesist this use of Faith only by attempting to overbid with Willpower. If she currently

_ Possesses at least twice as many Willpower Traits as the opposing character, she remains

unaffected. A character may attempt to repel multiple vampires wit ha group challenge.

¢ The Amaranth is usually a breach of the tradition of Destruction, which

prohibits the young from destroying the old. Evidence of diablerie might provoke

reprisals from other Cainites, although the exact reaction will depend on the

individual. Le xt al ion is may be called if the matter becomes too public, although:

craftier Cainites simply file the information for later use when they desire a boon

from the soul-sucker. The only circumstance under which Amaranth is sometimes

permitted is during a blood hunt.

It is much easier to lose Faith than gain it. Any time a character fails a test in

which True Faith was involved, he has a crisis of faith. During such a time, he is

one Trait down on all Social Challenges for the remainder of the scene and unable

to invoke his Faith again until his Faith has been restored (Narrator's discretion).

Che Gifts or Faith

True Faith grants more than strength of spirit and the ability to repel the

undead. The faithful are often revered for the touch of the holy upon the ra. What

follows area few potential effects — the Storyteller may decide to grant more as

the characters' faith grows.

* Certain legends speak of diablerists displaying some of the mannerisms:0

their victims, especially if their victim was especially strong-willed or of 

significantly lower generation than the diablerist. Naturally, clever Storytellers

will be quick to exploit this trait when dealing with habitual diablerists.

True Faith

The best defense the mortal world has against the Cainites is not fire to steel,

but rather faith. True Faith is a special power driven by the belief ina power, ehuly

consciousness —- sometimes even a purpose — that is greater than the bearer; and

as such, it is not limited to any particular religion. It is something that.must

constantly fed by habit and practiced every day. True Faith is possessed by few; ani

most mortals who have it are't very conscious of it because they focus on the got

¢ A character's True Faith rating may strengthen him against attempts to

influence his heart, mind or soul with supernatural powers, including Dominate and

Presence. This rating may cither be added to the difficulty of a Static Challenge or

be considered Traits up for the character. He may also add his Faith rating to his

Conscience rating when making a Conscience Challenge.

Faith rating just to gain one Blood Trait (so a vampire drinking from a nun with

True Faith 2 must de ink three Traits to gain one Blood Trait for his pool). Thése

of True Faith 3 or greater have blood that is considered harmful to Cainitesa -

Cainite suffers a level of aggravated damage for each Blood Trait he drinks, and-he

gains no benefits from the blood.

* The faithful can cast out malign influences from a person or place, such as"

in cases of ghostly or demonic possession. The character makes a Static Social"

Challenge (adding his Faith rating) against a difficulty listed on the accompanying"

table. This assumes that the character has the necessary trappings (salt, crucifixes,

holy water, et.). Without them, he is one Trait down in his challenge.

* The faithful may cure injury and sickness simply by their touch. The blessing

grants a number of health levels for healing purposes only. For example, a patient

who has suffered lethal wounds to his first Wounded level and received the healing

touch of a priest with True Faith 3, the patient is granted health levels until he

is considered Bruised for the purposes of healing time and making his Static

Physical Challenge to recover. (In this case, the patient need only rest For a day,

as if he were Bruised, and his difficulty is three Traits.) If the granted levels cause

the patient's health levels to rise above Bruised, he heals in a few hours. High et

than that, and the patient heals instantly. These effects last only until the patient

attempts to recover a health level (make the Static Physical Challenge). This

power cannot raise the dead, but it can save the dying, and it works with illness or

poison as well as physical injury.

* Some of the faithful are sensitive to the presence of evil. With a successful

Mental Challenge, the character may sense the presence of a vampire, ghost Of

demon. This challenge is not affected by Obfuscate, Obtenebration, the Stealth

Ability or other means of concealment. This effect is not conscious, so the faithful

say sense evil any time he is at peace, such as praying, reading, meditating or even

'sleeping. He may not sense evil presences while he is distracted {in a crowded

market or a feasting hall during a revel) or while occupied (in debate or combat).

The character does not know exactly what is near him, beyond something unholy.

"At Storyteller discretion, another Mental Challenge may provide insight.

Roll Items

The Faithful can bless items by undergoing religious ceremonies involving

those items. These scenes should be role played out, if a player's character is

involved. One item can be blessed over half an hour. Only one blessing per session

cat occur from a given character, and any given item can hold only one blessing

atatime. Blessed items have an additional Bonus Trait (Burning, Searing, Blinding,

Branding, Purging, Cleansing or Purifying) which may be be ina Physical Ch ai len ge

involving the item and in opposition to a supernatural creature. If the challenge

is lost, this Trait is gone, and the item loses its blessing. If the challenge is successful,

the item touches the creature, causing one level of aggravated damage (rather than

its normal damage). Bidding a normal Trait means that the item is being used as

normal, and it causes normal damage (if any).

Holy water will likewise cause aggravated damage -~ one level For a brief

splash, or one level per turn of inversion, although it does not gain any Bonus

Traits. Supernatural creatures handling such objects for extended periods of time

may cause them to lose their blessing.

This power is not completely limited to characters with True Faith. A devout,

honest priest hasa lesser version of this ability which allows him to bless holy water or the wine and wafers of communion.

Relics

Special holy items, relics of antiquity, hold further special powers, such as healing or blessing. These items are extremely rare, imbued with the faith of the masses over

the centuries, and the Storyteller must approve their entry into the game, as well as designate

their special abilities. They are usually kept within special protective cases called

reliquaries. Many medieval churches have at least one relic (sometimes of dubious

origin), Their power level varies according to the Faith surrounding them. The finger

bone of a little-known saint will hold less power than a splinter of the True Cross, for

example, Some charlatans peddle fake relics, such as animal bones for saints' bones,

tags for the mantle of the Virgin or splinters claimed to be fragments of the manger.

Strangely enough, however, these false relics may develop their own Faith rating and

some measure of holy power over time and with enough reverence of the faithful.

Not every relic is necessarily an ancient artifact, Holy water, the sacramental

wine and bread and the badges of pilgrims can have Faith ratings and act as relics.

Not every relic need be Christian -—- pagans, Jews and Muslims all have sacred

objects that are precious to them and their faithful. The exact effects of their relics

are up to the Storyteller.

The touch of a relic inflicts aggravated darn age on supernatural creatures and

causes them to flee if they fail a Courage Test against the level of damage they take.

This level is determined by the Storyteller, although a maximum of four levels is

Te commended. Other powers that the faithful may call upon from the relic should

represent the attributes for which the item or saint was revered. A sip from the Ha}

Grail will cure all illnesses, while a feather from the wing of Archangel Gabriel

might instill a great Leadership Ability on the owner. Unless desecrated, relic "ill

not lose its holy power. '

Holy Ground

Churches and other similar areas of faith (mosques, shrines, monasteries) may

have strong background of faithful resonance. Supernatural creatures are wary of much

regions. Varnpires must make a successful Courage check to enter such areas (thé

difficulty of which should be set by the Storyteller based on the level of Faith he deems

the property to hold). While they are in the area, they are considered one Trait down

on all challenges. Characters with True Faith are considered to have one Bonus Trait

on any challenge they make while on holy ground that is consecrated based on their

religious beliefs. All blood expenditures double while on holy ground, and perception:

affecting Disciplines (such as Auspex and Chimerstry) suffer a two-Trait penalty.

The ashen priests of the Road of Heaven supposedly know rites that allow

devout vampires to enter holy ground without discomfort, but they do not share

these with the unfaithful.

Scat us

Status is the central focus of many Cainites' existence. It represents the

amount of power and social prestige a character has within vampire society. Those

of lower status are expected to respect those of higher status. As one's status

increases, one is granted more respect and deference by other Cainites — and one

may become more of a target as well, Within a city, the prince typically (although

not always) has the most status. Neonates generally have little status, while

anarchs and most Caitiff have no status. In between are the power-hungry ancilla

and elders who continually jockey for positions in hopes of increasing their status.

The soc io-political manipulation of vampires by other vampires is governed,

in general, by set of unwritten rules that have been developed over centuries. The

weight of the ancients often holds the system in place as it is to their advantage to

ensure their hold over others is secure. Censure against those who buck the system

can be harsh, sometimes fatally so.

A character's status is represented by a list of Traits. These Status Traits are

awarded to the vampire for meritorious deeds or are indications of the value placed on

that vampire by the other Cainites of the city. Such awards (or the loss of regard) are

typically a matter of public knowledge and the awarding or stripping of Status is usually quickly spread through rumor or via public announcement at a gathering of Cainites.

Sample Status Traits include: Acknowledged, Admired, Adored, Cherished,

Esteemed, Exalted, Famous, Faultless, Feared, Honorable, Influential, Just, Praised;

Renowned, Respected, Revered and Trustworthy.

While the structure of Status has a rather codified set of rules, the dynamics of

the system should be role played. Status is a social convention. Vampires don't

actually keep a written list of which worthy holds such-and-such Status. Rather,

character's Status Traits hold role playing keys for how a character is perceived,

spoken of, and, to some degree, expected to behave. A Faultless character should

remain perceived as such, or no longer be worthy of the appellation, The gain and

_Joss-of Status is generally grist for the rumor mill, and any adjustment of someone's

Status should be spread, along with rumors as to the reason for the change, so that

alfcan share in their glory or humiliation, This is not to say that every little change

or expenditure of temporary Traits needs to be broadcast, but any vampire with the

Politics Ability can gauge the reputation (permanent Status Traits) of another

Cainite based on observing the overall reaction of others around her and the rumors

he may have heard of her. Consider it downtime small talk, if you will. A player can

expect a truthful answer when inquiring about a Cainite's Status in such a manner,

Gaining And Losing Status

In smaller games, the Storyteller is generally responsible for tracking the

Status of Cainites and deciding who is worthy of reward or scorn. Larger games usually allow the Storyteller to step back and allow the players to supervise such

matters, however, while maintaining an arbitration position in the case of debates.

Once released from the strict supervision of her sire and "accepted" by a prince,

in accordance with the tradition of Accounting, the new neonate acquires a single

Status Trait (usually Acknowledged, although her behavior during the period of

Accounting may merit a different Trait). Characters normally begin play with this

single Status Trait. During the course of the chronicle, the vampire gains or loses

Status according to her actions and the views of the other vampires around her.

Status is typically gained less frequently than it is lost. In order to gain status,

the Cainite must demonstrate to the other local vampires that he is worthy of

increased respect. In other words, it is often a matter of going beyond the "call of

duty." Simply obeying the Traditions and laws of the local prince is not enough —

every Cainite is expected to do as much. In addition to maintaining the reputation

that he already has (for example, remaining faultless and just, if he is considered

Faultless and Just), he must further demonstrate that he is worth additional regard.

The character must go beyond himself in the eyes of everyone.

Status can be lost For a multitude of reasons, such as making an enemy of an

elder, ignoring a boon or refusing to recognize another's status. Cainites can also

lose it because of breaking any of the Traditions or committing diablerie — if they

are caught and are still around, that is. Obviously, if you are going to commit any

of these actions, it is best to do so sans witnesses.

Note that a character may never gain more than one permanent Status Trait

per story. There is only one exception to this rule: if the prince awards or sanctions

an additional Trait. A character can lose any amount of Status Traits at any time

— the bigger they are, the harder they can fall.

Using Status

There are two types of Status Trait: temporary and permanent.

Permanent Status is recorded on the character sheet, and loss or gain. of such

is recorded directly on the sheet. It represents actual standing in Cainite society.

No matter how much temporary Status has been used during the course of a story,

it has no effect on the permanent total. A Cainite can use all of her temporary

Status Traits and still be considered to hold all of her standing.

=)

Permanent Status is a measure of character's credibility. In any situation where.

there is an open debate between Cainites (one Cainite's word against another):

Status is used as a determining factor. The same is true in the case of accusing another

of a crime for which there is no concrete evidence. In all such cases, the character

with the most Status is the one whose word is accepted. If there is a tie in this case,

then the character holding the most temporary Status Traits holds the advantage:

Anyone of higher Status may remove permanent Status from those lower than

herself at a cost of one permanent Status Trait per Trait removed.

A group of lower-Status Cainites may strip permanent Status from one of their

clan that has higher Status by expending a group total of permanent Status equal

to their target's permanent Status.

Temporary Status Traits are directly related to permanent Status Traits and

carry the same Trait names. They may be represented by Status cards to keep track

of them more easily. Each card should record the Status Trait and which character

it belongs to. The loss or use of temporary Status Traits usually lasts only for the duration of the story, and the player can regain the use of all of his cards when the

next story begins.

If the Cainite is stripped of permanent Status, then the temporary Status Trait

directly related to it is removed from play, no matter who holds the Trait at present.

If another vampire currently possesses that Trait, the borrower must approach the

vampire For a replacement Trait.

Example: Kharkas is considered by Cainite society to be Acknowledged, Feared

and Trustworthy. These are his permanent Status Traits and are recorded as such on his

sheet. At the beginning of the game, the player holds three temporary Status cards, each

with the name of Kharkas and the name of the Trait. Even if Kharkas expends or loans

away all of his cards, he is still considered to have three permanent Status Traits. [f he is

lowered in standing (Trustworthy is stripped for him breaking his word to the prince),

then the corresponding temporary Status card is also lost.

Following are examples of the use of Status Traits in the course of play. The

expenditure or use of a temporary Status Trait means that the character can no

longer use the card in play and should hand it to a Narrator.

A temporary Status Trait may be bid in a Social Challenge, in the place of 

Social Trait, if the situation is applicable. Temporary Status Traits can also be

expended to gain a Bonus Trait in a Social Challenge. If the Trait has been bid in

the challenge, it cannot also be used as a Bonus Trait. Any number of temporary

Traits can be expended in this way, as long as they are applicable to the challenge.

(Note: Temporary Status Traits cannot normally be used in challenges related to

Disciplines, unless all players are agreeable co such a use. If there is any doubt, the

Narrator makes the call.)

Anyone with more temporary Status Traits than another may remove

temporary Status from that vampire at a cost of one temporary Status Trait per

temporary Trait removed.

If character has more than double the amount of permanent Status Traits

than another member of her clan, she may award a permanent Status Trait to her

clan mate at the cost of one temporary Status Trait.

The prince of city may award permanent Status Traits to any vampire at the

cost of one temporary Status Trait per permanent Trait awarded. If he wishes to

award more than three permanent Status Traits to single Cainite in a single story,

the fourth and subsequent Traits will cost the prince permanent Status Traits

instead. He can also remove permanent Status from any other vampire for events

that occurred in his domain for the same cost.

A Cainite can show her favor or support of another vampire by loaning him

at emp ora ry Status Trait, although the recipient must return the Trait immediately

upon the asking. While he carries that Trait, he may use it as one of his own, after

which it is gone for the duration of the story, returning to the benefactor at the

beginning of the next story. A vampire may loan only one Trait to any given

vampire in this fashion, although she can make one-Trait loans to multiple

different vampires. This is the only way a character's temporary Status can time

above her permanent Status rating, barring Prestation.

A vampire must possess at least one Trait of Status (permanent or temporary,

even if the temporary Trait has been loaned by someone else, or is due to holding

boon) to petition the prince for any reason, such as asking for feeding grounds or

accusing someone of a crime.

Ignoring Status

A vampire can choose to ignore another vampire's Status in general ora 

specific use of Status against her. For example, a player may refuse to accept the bid

of Status Trait ina challenge where Status would normally be acceptable as a bid,

ore could refuse to allow Traits to be added from his opponent's temporary Status

Traits in the case of a tie. By doing so, however, she risks the wrath of society.

The structure of Status has been followed for so long, it has become a precious

social convention, and by ignoring the Status of a single vampire, she symbolically

ignores the Status of all vampires. This can result in the loss of her own permanent

standing if rumor of the offense spreads, especially if the offended Cainite is of

higher Status than she. Should she be disrespecting the standing of a lesser Cainite,

the loss of her standing may only be temporary (that is, the loss of a temporary

Status Trait for the duration of the story).

Outcasts And Status

It would seem, on the surface, that the Autarkis and Furor es have little to

worry about. Given that they do not follow the mainstream society and have little

regard for these social systems, they have no Status to lose. Some outsider groups

do have their own versions of Status, often variations on the mainstream, but equally likely co simply be a pecking order. Having no Status often hurts more than

it helps, though.

Elders almost never grant favors to outsiders, ra rely give them the benefit of the

dou be and are much more likely to vent their full wrath on them. It isso much simpler

(and more socially acceptable) to pick on those who have no political backing.

Additionally, it is not uncommon For a prince to turn a blind eye to them under

of a Cainite with no standing, even if such a murder is done in breach of the Second tradition. A Cainite who has shunned the normal protections of social convention

has clearly decided that he can survive without the benefits of civilization, whether

Although paying lip service to the elders has its price and binds you

vampires into a twisted dynamic structure of debt and unpredictable's "al of
